volumeTitle: Antigone. The Women of Trachis. Philoctetes. Oedipus at Colonus
label: Philoctetes (Section 1 - Section 130)

sections.label: Section 1
sections.original: ἀκτὴ μὲν ἥδε τῆς περιρρύτου χθονὸς
sections.translation: This is the headland of sea-washed Lemnos , land untrodden by men and desolate . It was here , child bred of the man who was the noblest of the Greeks , Neoptolemus son of Achilles , that I exposed

sections.label: Section 5
sections.original: Ποίαντος υἱὸν ἐξέθηκʼ ἐγώ ποτε ,
sections.translation: long ago the native of Malis , Poeas ’ son , on the express command of the two chieftains to do so , because his foot was all running with a gnawing disease . Neither libation nor burnt sacrifice could be attempted by us in peace , but with his wild , ill-omened cries

sections.label: Section 10
sections.original: κατεῖχʼ ἀεὶ πᾶν στρατόπεδον δυσφημίαις ,
sections.translation: he filled the whole camp continually with shrieking , moaning . But what need is there to speak of that ? The time is not ripe for too many words , lest he even learn that I am here , and I so waste the whole ruse whereby I think soon to take him .

sections.label: Section 15
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ἔργον ἤδη σὸν τὰ λοίφʼ ὑπηρετεῖν
sections.translation: Come , it is your task to serve as my ally in what remains , and to seek where in this region there is a cave with two mouths . During cold weather it provides two seats facing the sun , while in summer a breeze wafts sleep through the tunnelled chamber .

sections.label: Section 20
sections.original: βαιὸν δʼ ἔνερθεν ἐξ ἀριστερᾶς τάχʼ ἂν
sections.translation: And a little below , on the left hand , perhaps , you will see water rising from a spring , if it has not failed . Go there silently , and signal to me whether he still dwells in this same place , or is to be found elsewhere , so that the rest of my plan may be explained by me , heard by you ,

sections.label: Section 25
sections.original: ἐγὼ δὲ φράζω , κοινὰ δʼ ἐξ ἀμφοῖν ἴῃ .
sections.translation: and sped by the joint effort of us both .

sections.label: Section 26
sections.original: ἄναξ Ὀδυσσεῦ , τοὔργον οὐ μακρὰν λέγεις ·
sections.translation: King Odysseus , the completion of the task that you set me is not far off , for I believe I see a cave like that which you have described .

sections.label: Section 28
sections.original: ἄνωθεν ἢ κάτωθεν ; οὐ γὰρ ἐννοῶ .
sections.translation: Above you , or below ? I do not see it .

sections.label: Section 29
sections.original: τόδʼ ἐξύπερθε · καὶ στίβου γʼ οὐδεὶς κτύπος .
sections.translation: Here , high up — and of footfalls there is not a sound .

sections.label: Section 30
sections.original: ὅρα καθʼ ὕπνον μὴ καταυλισθεὶς κυρεῖ .
sections.translation: See that he is not sheltered there asleep .

sections.label: Section 31
sections.original: ὁρῶ κενὴν οἴκησιν ἀνθρώπων δίχα .
sections.translation: I see an empty dwelling , without occupants .

sections.label: Section 32
sections.original: οὐδʼ ἔνδον οἰκοποιός ἐστί τις τροφή ;
sections.translation: And is there no provision inside for human habitation ?

sections.label: Section 33
sections.original: στιπτή γε φυλλὰς ὡς ἐναυλίζοντί τῳ .
sections.translation: There is — a bed of leaves , as if for some one who makes his lodging here .

sections.label: Section 34
sections.original: τὰ δʼ ἄλλʼ ἔρημα , κοὐδέν ἐσθʼ ὑπόστεγον ;
sections.translation: And all else is bare ? There is nothing else beneath the roof ?

sections.label: Section 35
sections.original: αὐτόξυλόν γʼ ἔκπωμα , φλαυρουργοῦ τινος
sections.translation: Just a cup of bare wood , the masterpiece of a sorry craftsman , and with it these tools for kindling .

sections.label: Section 37
sections.original: κείνου τὸ θησαύρισμα σημαίνεις τόδε .
sections.translation: His is the store that you describe .

sections.label: Section 38
sections.original: ἰοὺ ἰού · καὶ ταῦτά γʼ ἄλλα θάλπεται
sections.translation: Ha ! Yes , and here besides are some rags drying in the sun , stained by some severe infection .

sections.label: Section 40
sections.original: ἁνὴρ κατοικεῖ τούσδε τοὺς τόπους σαφῶς ,
sections.translation: The man inhabits these regions , clearly , and is somewhere not far off . How could he go far afield when his foot is maimed by that old plague ? No , he has gone out in quest of food , or of some soothing herb that he may have noted somewhere .

sections.label: Section 45
sections.original: τὸν οὖν παρόντα πέμψον εἰς κατασκοπήν ,
sections.translation: Send your attendant , therefore , to keep watch , lest he come upon me unawares , since he would rather take me than all the Greeks together .

sections.label: Section 48
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ἔρχεταί τε καὶ φυλάξεται στίβος .
sections.translation: The man is going , and the path will be watched . And now , if you need anything else , say so . Exit Attendant , on the spectators ’ left .

sections.label: Section 50
sections.original: Ἀχιλλέως παῖ , δεῖ σʼ ἐφʼ οἷς ἐλήλυθας
sections.translation: Son of Achilles , you must be loyal to the goals of your mission — and not with your body alone . Should you hear some new plan unknown to you till now , you must serve it , since it is to serve that you are here .

sections.label: Section 54
sections.original: τί δῆτʼ ἄνωγας ;
sections.translation: Then what are your orders ?

sections.label: Section 54a
sections.original: τὴν Φιλοκτήτου σε δεῖ
sections.translation: You must cheat the mind of Philoctetes by means of a story

sections.label: Section 55
sections.original: ψυχὴν ὅπως δόλοισιν ἐκκλέψεις λέγων .
sections.translation: told as you converse with him . When he asks you who and from where you are , say that you are the son of Achilles — it is not in that detail that you will cheat him . But tell him you are sailing homeward , and have left the fleet of the Achaean warriors , after coming to hate them with unbounded hatred .

sections.label: Section 60
sections.original: οἵ σʼ ἐν λιταῖς στείλαντες ἐξ οἴκων μολεῖν ,
sections.translation: Give him this reason : when , with no other hope of taking Ilium , they had summoned you by their prayers to come from home , they judged you not worthy of the arms of Achilles , not worthy to receive them — even though you had come and were claiming them by right — but instead handed them over to Odysseus . Say what you

sections.label: Section 65
sections.original: θέλῃς καθʼ ἡμῶν ἔσχατʼ ἐσχάτων κακά .
sections.translation: will of me — even the vilest of vile insults . You will not harm me at all by that . But if you fail to do as I say , you will inflict pain on all the Argives , for if that man ’ s bow is not seized , you can never sack the realm of Dardanus .

sections.label: Section 70
sections.original: ὡς δʼ ἔστʼ ἐμοὶ μὲν οὐχί , σοὶ δʼ ὁμιλία
sections.translation: And learn why your intercourse with him may be free from mistrust and danger , while mine cannot . You have sailed to Troy under no oath to any man , nor under any constraint . Neither did you have any part in the earlier expedition . I , however , can deny none of these things . Accordingly , if he

sections.label: Section 75
sections.original: ὥστʼ εἴ με τόξων ἐγκρατὴς αἰσθήσεται ,
sections.translation: perceives me while he is still master of his bow , I am dead , and you , as my comrade , will share my doom . No , the thing for which we must devise a ruse is just this : how you may steal his invincible weapons . Well I know , my son , that by nature you are not apt

sections.label: Section 80
sections.original: τοιαῦτα φωνεῖν μηδὲ τεχνᾶσθαι κακά ·
sections.translation: to utter or contrive such treachery . Yet knowing that victory is a sweet prize to gain , steel yourself to do it . Our honesty shall be displayed another time . Now , however , give yourself to me for one brief , shameless day , and then for the rest of time

sections.label: Section 85
sections.original: κέκλησο πάντων εὐσεβέστατος βροτῶν .
sections.translation: may you be called the most righteous of all humankind .

sections.label: Section 86
sections.original: ἐγὼ μὲν οὓς ἂν τῶν λόγων ἀλγῶ κλύων ,
sections.translation: I abhor acting on advice , son of Laertes , which causes pain in the hearing . It is not in my nature to achieve anything by means of evil cunning , nor was it , as I hear , in my father ’ s .

sections.label: Section 90
sections.original: ἀλλʼ εἴμʼ ἑτοῖμος πρὸς βίαν τὸν ἄνδρʼ ἄγειν
sections.translation: But I am ready to take the man by force and without treachery , since with the use of one foot only , he will not overcome so many of us in a struggle . And yet I was sent to assist you and am reluctant to be called traitor . Still I prefer , my king ,

sections.label: Section 95
sections.original: δρῶν ἐξαμαρτεῖν μᾶλλον ἢ νικᾶν κακῶς .
sections.translation: to fail when doing what is honorable than to be victorious in a dishonorable manner .

sections.label: Section 96
sections.original: ἐσθλοῦ πατρὸς παῖ , καὐτὸς ὢν νέος ποτὲ
sections.translation: Son of a father so noble , I , too , in my youth once had a slow tongue and an active hand . But now that I have come forth to the test , I see that the tongue , not action , is what masters everything among men .

sections.label: Section 100
sections.original: τί μʼ οὖν ἄνωγας ἄλλο πλὴν ψευδῆ λέγειν ;
sections.translation: What , then , are your orders — apart from my lying ?

sections.label: Section 101
sections.original: λέγω σʼ ἐγὼ δόλῳ Φιλοκτήτην λαβεῖν .
sections.translation: I command you to take Philoctetes by deceit .

sections.label: Section 102
sections.original: τί δʼ ἐν δόλῳ δεῖ μᾶλλον ἢ πείσαντʼ ἄγειν ;
sections.translation: And why by deceit rather than by persuasion ?

sections.label: Section 103
sections.original: οὐ μὴ πίθηται · πρὸς βίαν δʼ οὐκ ἂν λάβοις .
sections.translation: He will never listen ; and by force you cannot take him .

sections.label: Section 104
sections.original: οὕτως ἔχει τι δεινὸν ἰσχύος θράσος ;
sections.translation: Has he strength so terrific to make him bold ?

sections.label: Section 105
sections.original: ἰούς γʼ ἀφύκτους καὶ προπέμποντας φόνον .
sections.translation: Yes , shafts inevitable , escorts of death .

sections.label: Section 106
sections.original: οὐκ ἆρʼ ἐκείνῳ γʼ οὐδὲ προσμῖξαι θρασύ ;
sections.translation: Then one does not dare even approach him ?

sections.label: Section 107
sections.original: οὔ , μὴ δόλῳ λαβόντα γʼ , ὡς ἐγὼ λέγω .
sections.translation: No , unless he takes the man by deceit , as I prescribe .

sections.label: Section 108
sections.original: οὐκ αἰσχρὸν ἡγεῖ δῆτα τὸ ψευδῆ λέγειν ;
sections.translation: Then you think it brings no shame to speak what is false ?

sections.label: Section 109
sections.original: οὔκ , εἰ τὸ σωθῆναί γε τὸ ψεῦδος φέρει .
sections.translation: No , not if the falsehood yields deliverance .

sections.label: Section 110
sections.original: πῶς οὖν βλέπων τις ταῦτα τολμήσει λακεῖν ;
sections.translation: And with what expression on his face will anyone dare mouth those lies ?

sections.label: Section 111
sections.original: ὅταν τι δρᾷς εἰς κέρδος , οὐκ ὀκνεῖν πρέπει .
sections.translation: When what you do promises gain , it is wrong to shrink back .

sections.label: Section 112
sections.original: κέρδος δʼ ἐμοὶ τί τοῦτον ἐς Τροίαν μολεῖν ;
sections.translation: And what gain is it for me that he should come to Troy ?

sections.label: Section 113
sections.original: αἱρεῖ τὰ τόξα ταῦτα τὴν Τροίαν μόνα .
sections.translation: His arrows alone will capture Troy .

sections.label: Section 114
sections.original: οὐκ ἆρʼ ὁ πέρσων , ὡς ἐφάσκετʼ , εἴμʼ ἐγώ ;
sections.translation: Then I am not to be the conqueror , as you said ?

sections.label: Section 115
sections.original: οὔτʼ ἂν σὺ κείνων χωρὶς οὔτʼ ἐκεῖνα σοῦ .
sections.translation: Neither will you be without them , nor they without you .

sections.label: Section 116
sections.original: θηρατέʼ οὖν γίγνοιτʼ ἄν , εἴπερ ὧδʼ ἔχει .
sections.translation: It would seem , then , that we must track them down , if things stand as you say .

sections.label: Section 117
sections.original: ὡς τοῦτό γʼ ἔρξας δύο φέρει δωρήματα .
sections.translation: Know that by doing this task , you win two rewards .

sections.label: Section 118
sections.original: ποίω ; μαθὼν γὰρ οὐκ ἂν ἀρνοίμην τὸ δρᾶν .
sections.translation: What are they ? If I knew , I would not refuse the deed .

sections.label: Section 119
sections.original: σοφός τʼ ἂν αὑτὸς κἀγαθὸς κεκλῇʼ ἅμα .
sections.translation: You will be celebrated in the same breath as clever and as noble .

sections.label: Section 120
sections.original: ἴτω · ποήσω , πᾶσαν αἰσχύνην ἀφείς .
sections.translation: So be it ! I will do it , and cast off all shame .

sections.label: Section 121
sections.original: ἦ μνημονεύεις οὖν ἅ σοι παρῄνεσα ;
sections.translation: Do you remember , then , the story that I recommended ?

sections.label: Section 122
sections.original: σάφʼ ἴσθʼ , ἐπείπερ εἰσάπαξ συνῄνεσα .
sections.translation: Be sure of it , since once and for all I have consented .

sections.label: Section 123
sections.original: σὺ μὲν μένων νυν κεῖνον ἐνθάδʼ ἐκδέχου ,
sections.translation: You stay here , then , to wait for him . Meanwhile I will go away , so as not to be observed here with you ,

sections.label: Section 125
sections.original: καὶ τὸν σκοπὸν πρὸς ναῦν ἀποστελῶ πάλιν .
sections.translation: and I will send our lookout back to your ship . And , if in my view you seem to linger at all beyond the due time , I will send that same man back again , after disguising him as the captain of a merchant-ship , so that secrecy may be on our side .

sections.label: Section 130
sections.original: οὗ δῆτα , τέκνον , ποικίλως αὐδωμένου
sections.translation: Then , son , as he tells his artful story , take whatever in his tale is from time to time helpful to you . Now I will go to the ship , leaving matters here to you . May escorting Hermes the Deceiver , lead us on , and divine Victory , Athena Polias , who saves me always ! Exit Odysseus , on the spectators ’ left .
